course_description:
The course will require students to analyse policy arrangements and practical applications of hazard and disaster management in local, state and national government contexts.This course describes where & how interacting environmental & social systems create distinctive landscapes, regions & places at risk from environmental hazards. Determinants of community exposure, risk & resilience to hazards are identified & analysed from a geographical perspective, as are society's attempts to manage & adapt to hazards.

Natural hazards are significant agents of damage, disruption and loss across diverse local and global landscapes. Despite the name, natural hazards are not entirely natural. Understanding earth system processes and the frequency of these hazards is essential in developing prevention and mitigation measures to reduce hazard risk. This course will introduce the naturalﾠsystems that create hazards, including floods, cyclones, earthquakes, tsunamis, fires, and droughts; so as to investigateﾠwhere and why the interplay of environmental and social systems yields spatially varying exposures to hazard risk, and contrast choices of risk management and adaptation. ﾠ

Attention to emergency and disaster planning is now a requirement across many professional areas (e.g. in state and local governments, education, and aid agencies) as it has become embedded in the wider operations of governments, private enterprises and the public to create sustainable, “prepared communities”. Hazard geography, with its integrating, spatial perspective offers a robust framework with which to combine and apply expertise from the environmental and social sciences, spatial sciences and planning in practical, problem-solving contexts. ﾠﾠ

External courses are delivered entirely online, and students must participate online for learning and assessment.

Note: students may be required to sit exams at a UQ campus or an approved off-campus exam centre. Please refer to the Assessment tab details for information on exam attendance requirements.

UQ Public Timetable

Additional timetable information
This mode is only for external students. Internal students should take GEOS7106 Internal version to gain hands-on experience in lab and field activities.
In this external course, students are expected to watch all lecture recordings and practical recordings in their own time. Students need to attend three 3-hour online (live-streamed, interactive) practical sessions throughout the semester.
course_aims: This Course aims to investigate: (1) the geologic and atmospheric processes that cause environmental hazards; (2) the distribution and attributes of hazards in Australia and internationally; (3) patterns and determinants of exposure, risk, vulnerability and resilience to hazards; and (4) how people perceive, manage and adjust to hazardous environments. Practical understanding of hazard geography is promoted using robust conceptual and skills frameworks.

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate knowledge of a range of natural hazards.
Research, interpret and synthesize information from a range of sources relevant to the analyses of hazards, risks, vulnerability, resilience, adaptation and management.

Analyse information to gain understanding of hazards and the landscapes in which they exist and thereby allow critical assessments of hazards and approaches to dealing with them i.e. adaptation, mitigation or avoidance; and how to undertake appropriate decision-making.
Communicate knowledge, interpretations and analyses in written, graphical and oral formats effectively and in a manner appropriate to the field.
Understand and use the language, frameworks and tools used in hazard geography and emergency management.
Work cooperatively in a small group to deliver a natural hazards risk assessment report and to make recommendations to stakeholders on risk reduction.

assessment_summary.category: Role play/ Simulation, Tutorial/ Problem Set
assessment_summary.title: Practical worksheets and role play

assessment_details.title: Practical worksheets and role play
assessment_details.category: Role play/ Simulation, Tutorial/ Problem Set
assessment_details.task_description: There are four worksheets to complete throughout the semester. Practical recordings will be available to guide students to complete these worksheets. Exercises contain a mix of short questions and brief essays. The exercises are designed to consolidate learning. Questions in the exercises may appear in the final exam. Worksheet 1: Week 4 practical recording on Floods; submit in Week 5 Worksheet 2: Week 5 practical recording on ENSO; submit in Week 6 (day before Easter holiday) Worksheet 3: Week 8 online live-streamed practical on Earthquakes; submit in Week 9 Worksheet 4: Week 9 practical recording on Volcano/GIS; submit in Week 10 All worksheets are to be submitted by Friday 16:00 of the week following the respective prac. (except for Worksheet 2: submit on Thursday because Friday is a public holiday) Students should attend the third online live-streamed practical on "Managing a Disaster" to participate in the role-play activity. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Download the worksheets from Blackboard, answer all the questions, then submit electronically on Blackboard.
Students must attend the third online live-streamed practical on "Managing a Disaster" to participate in the role-play activity.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Extension is not available for the roleplay assessment. Do not submit an extension request for this component.
This assessment may only be eligible for alternative arrangement if exceptional circumstances arise on the day of the scheduled presentation. Please contact your course coordinator with supporting documentation of the exceptional circumstances that prevented you from attending for consideration of an alternate arrangement.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
You are required to submit assessable items on time. If you fail to meet the submission deadline for any assessment item, then 10% of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item (the assessment ‘marked from’ value) will be deducted as a late penalty for every day (or part day) late after the due date.
For example, if you submit your assignment 1 hour late, you will be penalised 10%; if your assignment is 24.5 hours late, you will be penalised 20% (because it is late by one 24-hour period plus part of another 24-hour period).

assessment_details.title: Risk assessment report
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessed. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: The risk assessment report is a GROUP assignment. Students will work in groups to assess the natural hazard risk in a local suburb. Results will need to be collated and synthesised as a written report. Instructions and guiding questions for discussions will be announced in the first online practical and posted on Blackboard. Students can form their own groups during the practical. Absentees will be grouped randomly. The report should be no more than 5500 words, exclusive of references. 10% of the final mark will be deducted for every 10% excess words. Final marks will be distributed among group members based on the peer assessment scores. The peer assessment form should be submitted individually to Blackboard ("submission" folder) by the same due date. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
This group report should be submitted electronically via Turnitin on Blackboard (1 submission per group)
Each student can submit an anonymous peer assessment form, also via Blackboard, by the due date. The form can be downloaded from Blackboard. Submission of peer assessment is optional.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
See the Additional assessment information section further below for information relating to extension and deferral applications.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
You are required to submit assessable items on time. If you fail to meet the submission deadline for any assessment item, then 10% of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item (the assessment ‘marked from’ value) will be deducted as a late penalty for every day (or part day) late after the due date.
For example, if you submit your assignment 1 hour late, you will be penalised 10%; if your assignment is 24.5 hours late, you will be penalised 20% (because it is late by one 24-hour period plus part of another 24-hour period).

assessment_details.title: Research progress in hazard geography
assessment_details.task_description: This is an individual assessment item. Students will each select a peer-reviewed journal article on the theme of environmental hazards, published in the past ten years. The article may be related to Climate change projections, Natural hazard trends, or Hazard vulnerability (just some examples). Analyse the article critically and then record a presentation (slides + webcam) covering the section below. The presentation should be no more than 15 minutes. 1. Demonstrate your understanding of the article (weighting: 30%) Synthesise the work by explaining the aims, significance, method, and major findings of the research. Explain how the research and results contribute to hazard risk reduction or the progress of hazard geography 2. Evaluate the article (20%) Critically review the article, e.g. give your comments on the hypotheses, methods, or analyses of the work, based on your knowledge in hazard science or/and disaster management. Give your opinions on how the research or this paper can be improved 3. Relate the research or concepts presented in this paper to the topics in this course and/or to other courses in your program (20%) How does this article fit (or contradict) with the knowledge you have learned so far? How can you apply the knowledge? Would you recommend this paper to future students taking this course? Why/why not? 4. Design a research project relevant to this paper, pitch the idea in the final part of the presentation (30%) Describe your project idea, including the motivation, aims, research questions/hypotheses, and explain how such a research project will contribute to the progress the field of hazard-related research. The second live-streamed practical is a workshop on how to choose a suitable article for this assessment, and how to construct a video presentation for this assignment. Students are to attend this practical to discuss the selection of an article with the course coordinator in person during this practical. The video presentation must be presented by the student (not an AI avatar or similar). A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Student will create a video (PowerPoint slides by screen sharing + voice-over + webcam). This can be created using Zoom or Powerpoint. Please submit your video on Padlet AND a submission form via Blackboard.

See the Additional assessment information section further below for information relating to extension and deferral applications.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
You are required to submit assessable items on time. If you fail to meet the submission deadline for any assessment item, then 10% of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item (the assessment ‘marked from’ value) will be deducted as a late penalty for every day (or part day) late after the due date.
For example, if you submit your assignment 1 hour late, you will be penalised 10%; if your assignment is 24.5 hours late, you will be penalised 20% (because it is late by one 24-hour period plus part of another 24-hour period).

assessment_details.title: End-of-semester Exam
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: The examination questions will be based on materials covered in the whole course, including lectures, practical sessions, and from the suggested readings. Details of the exam will be advised in Mid-May. This exam requires attendance on campus, or at an approved off-campus exam centre. You must be able to attend one of these venues. Please refer to the Off-Campus exams information page for further information. Please note a digital exam is not available for this assessment. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.exam_details: Planning time 10 minutes Duration 180 minutes Calculator options (In person) Casio FX82 series only or UQ approved and labelled calculator Open/closed book Closed book examination - specified written materials permitted Materials One A4 sheet of handwritten notes , single sided, is permitted Exam platform Paper based Invigilation Invigilated in person

You may be able to defer this exam .
See the Additional assessment information section further below for information relating to extension and deferral applications.

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Weekly lecture Lectures will be recorded (i.e. no live stream). See 4.2 below for the detailed schedule. Lecture topics include: Introduction of environmental hazards Risk assessment and perception Floods Coastal storms Droughts Bushfires Bushfire modelling Earthquakes and tsunamis Disaster planning Disaster response Disaster recovery QLD disaster coordination structure Resilience and hazards in the future Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 2 To Week 11 (02 Mar - 17 May)
learning_activities.topic: Practicals This external course is catered for external students who may have to work full-time outside of their studies. Therefore, only three live-streamed practicals are timetabled. Learning materials (recordings, worksheets) for other practical sessions will be available on Blackboard, students are expected to complete all required worksheets and group work in their own time. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library .
